figures.caption: FIG. 9. Anomalous Hall conductivity σxy across all four topological phases. Top panel: overlay of all four phases as a function of Fermi energy EF /v. Dotted horizontal lines mark ±e2/h. Individual panels: (a) QSH phase (C = 0, A0 = 0.125, θ = 0, ∆ = 0.377v); (b) Chern phase (C = −1, A0 = 0.625, θ = 0.829 rad, ∆ = 0.002v); (c) Chern phase (C = +1, A0 = 0.625, θ = 0.742 rad, ∆ = 0.002v); (d) trivial phase (C = 0, A0 = 1.075, θ = 0, ∆ = 0.137v). Blue shading marks the insulating gap in each panel. The Chern phases develop near-quantized values σxy → Ce2/h in the gap, while the QSH and trivial phases show vanishing and non-quantized responses, respectively.

FIG. 4. (a-c) Anomalous Hall conductivity σxy at T = 0 as a function of Fermi energy. plot in (a) shows σxy for the trivial AM; even though no topological transition occurs, σxy develops a non-trivial energy dependence with increasing A0 as the broken C4zT symmetry allows Berry curvature to accumulate. Results in (b) shows the QSH AM; as A0 increases through Ac1 0 , a quantized plateau σxy = +e2/h emerges in the gap region, characteristic of the Chern insulating phase. The approach to the plateau is confirmed by the transition value shown by the dashed line. panel (c) verifies the exact even symmetry σxy(EF ) = σxy(−EF ) with residual RMS error 7.1 × 10−17 e2/h, confirming a symmetry ε → −ε (particle–hole-like) of the Berry curvature in this model. (d) Tracks the spin-resolved Chern numbers and the total σxy as a function of A0, unambiguously identifying the QSH (C = 0), Chern (C = −1), and trivial (C = 0) phases with critical amplitudes Ac1 0 = 0.521 and Ac2 0 = 0.918.

FIG. 8. Band structures and topological phase diagram of the QSH AM under LPL. (a)–(d): Spin-resolved band structure of the QSH AM (m = 3.8v, b = −v, ta = √ 3) at A0 = 0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0 (θ = 0). Spin-up (solid teal) and spin-down (dashed orange) bands evolve differently; the spin-down gap closes at Ac1 0 producing the Chern phase, while the spin-up gap closes at Ac2 0 . Insets show ∆↑ and ∆↓. Lower panels: spin Chern numbers C↑, C↓, total C, and spin Chern number Cs = (C↑ − C↓)/2 vs. A0.
TABLE I. Comparison of transport properties and topological phase transitions in the d-wave AM and conventional AFM under LPL irradiation (θ = 0, QSH initial phase).

FIG. 11. Anomalous thermal Hall conductivity κ (0) xy /T at T = 0 across all four topological phases. Top panel: overlay of all four phases; dotted lines at ±π2/3 k2 B/h mark the WF-predicted quantized values. Individual panels (a)–(d) use the same parameters as Fig. 9. The C = +1 Chern phase (c) shows near-quantization κ (0) xy /T ≈ +1.913 k2 B/h ≈ π2/3 k2 B/h, confirming the anomalous Wiedemann–Franz law κ (0) xy = −L0Tσxy. The sign of κ (0) xy /T tracks the sign of C, providing an unambiguous thermal Hall fingerprint of each topological phase.

FIG. 5. Anomalous Nernst conductivity αxy as a function of Fermi energy. In all panels the trivial AM is considered by given m = 4.2v, b = −v, ta = √ 3, θ = 0. (a) at A0 = 0.6 for six temperatures, demonstrating the thermally activated nature (αxy → 0 as T → 0). (b) Scaled quantity αxy/(kBT ) as a function of EF at A0 = 0.6; the Mott prediction −(π2kB/3e) ∂σxy/∂ε|µ (dashed) is approached as T → 0. (c) at kBT = 0.005v for four LPL amplitudes, showing the evolution of the Nernst response with light intensity. (d) Odd symmetry αxy(EF ) = −αxy(−EF ) verified for A0 = 0.6; Notice that a RMS error is 5.8× 10−17 ekB/h.

FIG. 10. Anomalous Nernst conductivity αxy across all four topological phases at kBT = 0.01v. Top panel: overlay of all four phases. Individual panels with the same parameter choices as Fig. 9. Noice that the dramatic enhancement in the Chern phases (b-c): peak values of ≈ 1.88 ekB/h compared to 0.015 ekB/h in the QSH phase (a), arising from the exponential sensitivity of αxy to the small gap ∆ ≈ 0.002v. The Nernst response provides a far more sensitive probe of topological gap size than the Hall conductivity.
FIG. 6. Physical conductivities as a function of the Polarization angle θ. All three conductivities (a-c) show a d-wave nature under LPL that is a remarkable result and a fingerprint for AM.

FIG. 7. Nernst and thermal conductivities in terms of chemical potential (a-c) Nernst conductivity as a function of chemical potential at different values of amplitude. (d-f) Thermal Hall conductivity as a function of chemical potential at different values of amplitude.
